title: Cloud Computing Basics
description: AWS, Azure, GCP fundamentals. Compute, storage, networking, serverless, containers, deployment.

questions.q: What is CLOUD COMPUTING?
options: On-demand delivery of computing resources (servers, storage, databases, networking, AI) over the internet — pay only for what you use
questions.explanation: The cloud replaced the old model of buying and maintaining physical servers. Resources spin up in seconds, scale dynamically with demand, and you pay only for usage. Has fundamentally reshaped how software is built and delivered globally.

questions.q: What are the MAJOR cloud providers?
options: AWS (Amazon), Azure (Microsoft), Google Cloud (GCP) — plus regional players like Alibaba Cloud, Oracle Cloud, IBM Cloud
questions.explanation: AWS is the market leader globally; Azure dominates enterprise (especially Microsoft-heavy shops); GCP leads in data/AI workloads. Many organizations use multiple ('multi-cloud') to avoid lock-in or match workloads to provider strengths.

questions.q: What's the DIFFERENCE between cloud and traditional on-premise?
options: On-prem: you buy/maintain physical hardware. Cloud: rent virtual resources, scale on demand, no capital expense
questions.explanation: On-prem requires upfront capital, ongoing maintenance, capacity planning, and absorbing failures yourself. Cloud shifts these to operating expense and outsources infrastructure ops. Trade-offs: ongoing cost, vendor dependency, but vastly faster iteration.

questions.q: What is ELASTICITY in cloud computing?
options: The ability to automatically scale resources up during traffic spikes and back down when demand drops — paying only for what's used
questions.explanation: Elasticity is one of cloud's superpowers. Your Black Friday traffic surge auto-provisions more servers; quiet Tuesday morning shrinks back. Try doing that with physical hardware. Designs that exploit elasticity ('cloud-native') unlock enormous efficiency.

questions.q: Why do MOST modern apps run in the cloud?
options: Speed to deploy, automatic scaling, global reach, managed services, lower upfront cost, no hardware management
questions.explanation: The cloud lets a solo developer deploy globally-distributed software in hours that would have required teams and months in the on-prem era. This compression of capability is why startups grew faster post-2010 than ever before in history.

questions.q: What does IAAS stand for?
options: Infrastructure as a Service — you rent virtual servers, networks, storage; manage the OS and everything above (e.g., AWS EC2)
questions.explanation: IaaS gives maximum flexibility but most operational responsibility. You pick the OS, install software, configure security. Examples: EC2 (AWS), Compute Engine (GCP), Azure VMs. Foundation underlying most other cloud services.

questions.q: What does PAAS stand for?
options: Platform as a Service — you deploy code; provider manages servers, OS, runtime (e.g., Heroku, Vercel, App Engine)
questions.explanation: PaaS removes infrastructure management. Push code; platform handles the rest. Examples: Heroku, Vercel, Netlify, Google App Engine, Azure App Service. Trade speed for flexibility — less control, but much faster to ship.

questions.q: What does SAAS stand for?
options: Software as a Service — fully managed apps you use via the browser (e.g., Gmail, Slack, Notion, Figma)
questions.explanation: SaaS is the most familiar cloud category for end-users. You don't think about servers or installations — you just log in and use software. Most modern business runs on SaaS rather than installed software.

questions.q: What is FAAS (Functions as a Service)?
options: Run small pieces of code in response to events without managing servers (e.g., AWS Lambda, Cloudflare Workers, Vercel Functions)
questions.explanation: FaaS / serverless lets you upload a function, and the cloud runs it on demand. Pay per execution, often pennies. Great for event-driven workloads, APIs, webhooks. Major efficiency for many use cases compared to always-running servers.

questions.q: How do you CHOOSE between IaaS, PaaS, and FaaS?
options: Match to control needs and complexity: more control = IaaS; faster delivery = PaaS; event-driven small workloads = FaaS
questions.explanation: Many apps mix all three: PaaS for the main web app, FaaS for background jobs, IaaS for specialized workloads. Use the highest level of abstraction that meets your needs — managing servers is rarely the business value you're trying to deliver.

questions.q: What is the PUBLIC CLOUD?
options: Resources shared on a provider's infrastructure (AWS, Azure, GCP) — accessible to anyone who signs up
questions.explanation: 'Public' means shared infrastructure, not public data. Resources are isolated per customer but run on the same physical hardware. This sharing is what makes public cloud economically efficient — massive scale enables low per-unit costs.

questions.q: What is a PRIVATE CLOUD?
options: Cloud infrastructure dedicated to one organization — either self-hosted or hosted by a provider for that org alone
Cloud only one person can see
questions.explanation: Private clouds suit highly regulated industries (finance, healthcare, defense) needing strict compliance, isolation, or sovereignty. Higher cost than public cloud; less elastic. Used when public cloud doesn't meet specific requirements.

questions.q: What is HYBRID CLOUD?
options: Combining public cloud with on-premise or private cloud — bridging both for specific workloads
questions.explanation: Common pattern: keep sensitive data on-prem, run scalable workloads in public cloud, connect them securely. Most large enterprises run hybrid. Tools (Azure Arc, AWS Outposts, GCP Anthos) help manage hybrid environments uniformly.

questions.q: What is MULTI-CLOUD?
options: Using multiple cloud providers for different workloads — to avoid lock-in, optimize cost, or leverage specific strengths
questions.explanation: Multi-cloud increases complexity (each provider has different APIs, tools, billing). Benefits: vendor leverage, redundancy, picking best-of-breed services. Many organizations end up multi-cloud through acquisitions and developer choice rather than strategy.

questions.q: Which DEPLOYMENT MODEL is most common for startups?
options: Public cloud — fastest to start, lowest upfront cost, scales with growth
questions.explanation: Public cloud's low barrier to entry transformed startup economics. A pre-revenue startup can deploy production-grade infrastructure globally for under $100/month. Same setup would have required hundreds of thousands of dollars in the pre-cloud era.

questions.q: What is a VIRTUAL MACHINE in the cloud?
options: An emulated computer running on shared physical hardware — your own OS, your own software, isolated from other VMs
questions.explanation: VMs (AWS EC2, Azure Virtual Machines, GCP Compute Engine) are the foundational cloud compute service. Pick CPU, RAM, storage, OS — boot in minutes. Pay by the hour or second. Most cloud services run on VMs under the hood.

questions.q: What is a CONTAINER?
options: A lightweight package containing an application and its dependencies — runs consistently across any environment
questions.explanation: Containers (Docker is the dominant format) solved 'works on my machine' problems. They include the runtime, libraries, and code, but share the host OS kernel. Much lighter than full VMs. Foundation of modern cloud-native applications.

questions.q: What is KUBERNETES?
options: An open-source platform for orchestrating containers at scale — automating deployment, scaling, and management
questions.explanation: Kubernetes (K8s) became the de facto standard for running production container workloads. Complex but powerful. Managed offerings (EKS on AWS, AKS on Azure, GKE on GCP) hide much of the operational complexity.

questions.q: What is SERVERLESS?
options: A model where you write code and the platform handles all server provisioning, scaling, and maintenance — you pay only for execution time
questions.explanation: Serverless (AWS Lambda, Cloudflare Workers, Vercel Functions, Google Cloud Run) doesn't mean 'no servers' — it means 'no servers you manage'. Perfect for event-driven, sporadic, or bursty workloads. Can scale to zero (no idle cost).

questions.q: When should you CHOOSE serverless over a traditional server?
options: Variable/bursty traffic, event-driven workloads, low-traffic APIs, when you don't want to manage servers — and pay only for actual usage
questions.explanation: Serverless shines for irregular workloads. A traditional always-on server costs the same whether handling 1 or 1000 requests/hour. Serverless costs scale with traffic. But for steady high-traffic apps, traditional servers are often cheaper.

questions.q: What is OBJECT STORAGE?
options: Storing files (images, videos, backups) as objects in flat namespaces — virtually infinite scale, low cost per GB
questions.explanation: Object storage (AWS S3, Azure Blob, Google Cloud Storage) is the workhorse of the cloud. Stores trillions of objects globally. Backups, user uploads, static websites, data lakes — all common use cases. Often the first cloud service developers learn.

questions.q: What is AWS S3?
options: Simple Storage Service — Amazon's object storage; the de facto industry standard since 2006
questions.explanation: S3 popularized object storage. 'S3-compatible' became a standard — many other providers (Cloudflare R2, Backblaze B2, MinIO) offer S3-compatible APIs. Learning S3 transfers to many environments.

questions.q: What is a BUCKET in S3 / object storage?
options: A logical container holding objects — analogous to a top-level folder, globally unique name
questions.explanation: Bucket names are globally unique across all of S3. Buckets have policies (access control), encryption settings, lifecycle rules. Misconfigured buckets (especially public ones) have caused many data breaches — careful configuration is essential.

questions.q: What is BLOCK STORAGE?
options: Persistent disk-like storage attached to VMs — operates as a hard drive (e.g., AWS EBS, Azure Managed Disks)
questions.explanation: Block storage attaches to one VM at a time. Like a virtual hard drive. Used for OS disks and application data needing low-latency, random access. Different from object storage (designed for files) and file storage (network-shared).

questions.q: What is CDN (Content Delivery Network)?
options: A globally distributed network of servers caching content close to users — dramatically faster delivery worldwide
questions.explanation: CDNs (Cloudflare, AWS CloudFront, Fastly, Akamai) reduce latency by serving content from servers near users. A static asset served from Singapore loads faster for a Sydney user than from Virginia. Essential for any globally accessed site.

questions.q: What is MANAGED DATABASE service?
options: A database where the cloud provider handles backups, patching, scaling, replication — you focus on data, not operations
questions.explanation: Examples: AWS RDS, Azure SQL Database, Google Cloud SQL. Significantly reduces operational burden. Worth the modest premium over self-hosted databases for nearly all teams — DB admin is a specialized skill.

questions.q: What is a RELATIONAL database service in the cloud?
options: Managed versions of traditional databases like PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQL Server — same query language, no maintenance
questions.explanation: AWS RDS, Azure SQL, GCP Cloud SQL all offer PostgreSQL and MySQL as managed services. Same engine you'd run on your own server, but the cloud handles backups, replicas, patches, and failover.

questions.q: What is NOSQL in the cloud?
options: Managed non-relational databases — document (MongoDB, DynamoDB), key-value (Redis), graph, time-series — each for specific patterns
questions.explanation: AWS DynamoDB, Azure Cosmos DB, MongoDB Atlas, Google Firestore are popular cloud NoSQL options. Designed for massive scale and flexible schemas. Best for specific patterns (key-value lookup, document storage, real-time apps), not as default replacement for SQL.

questions.q: What is a SERVERLESS DATABASE?
options: A database that automatically scales (including to zero) and bills by usage — examples: Neon, PlanetScale, Aurora Serverless, Supabase
questions.explanation: Serverless databases match cloud-native economics. Pay only for actual queries and storage. Scale to zero during quiet periods (saving money). Particularly attractive for indie developers and startups with variable traffic.

questions.q: What is a DATA WAREHOUSE in the cloud?
options: Specialized databases optimized for analytics on huge datasets — BigQuery, Snowflake, Redshift, Databricks
questions.explanation: Designed for OLAP (analytical) workloads, not OLTP (transactional). Process billions of rows in seconds. Modern data warehouses also enable AI/ML on the data. Most data analysis at scale runs on these tools.

questions.q: What is a VPC (Virtual Private Cloud)?
options: A logically isolated network within a cloud provider — your own slice of the cloud with control over IPs, subnets, routing
questions.explanation: Every serious cloud workload runs in a VPC. You define the network topology, security groups, routing. Foundation of cloud security. AWS VPC, Azure VNet, GCP VPC are conceptually similar.

questions.q: What is a LOAD BALANCER?
options: Distributes incoming traffic across multiple servers — provides scalability and high availability
questions.explanation: Load balancers (AWS ELB, Azure Load Balancer, GCP Cloud Load Balancing) sit in front of multiple servers, routing requests intelligently. Critical for high availability — if one server fails, others continue serving traffic.

questions.q: What is DNS in the cloud?
options: Domain Name System — translates domain names (example.com) to IP addresses. Cloud DNS (Route 53, Azure DNS, Cloud DNS) handles this at scale
questions.explanation: DNS is foundational internet plumbing. Cloud DNS services offer high availability, geographic routing, health checks, low latency. Often paired with CDNs for optimal global performance.

questions.q: What is API GATEWAY?
options: A managed service that handles API requests — authentication, rate limiting, routing, monitoring
questions.explanation: API Gateway (AWS, Azure, GCP all have versions) sits in front of backend services. Adds auth, throttling, caching, monitoring without each service implementing these. Common pattern for microservices and serverless APIs.

questions.q: What is a PRIVATE ENDPOINT?
options: Accessing cloud services through your private network instead of public internet — better security and performance
questions.explanation: Many enterprise deployments use private endpoints (PrivateLink in AWS, Private Endpoint in Azure) to keep traffic off the public internet. Reduces attack surface and meets compliance requirements.

questions.q: What is IAM (Identity and Access Management)?
options: The system controlling WHO (users, services) can do WHAT (actions) on WHICH resources in your cloud
questions.explanation: IAM is the foundation of cloud security. Misconfigured IAM is the cause of many cloud breaches. Each major cloud has its own IAM (AWS IAM, Azure AD/Entra ID, Google Cloud IAM). Mastering IAM is essential for any cloud role.

questions.q: What is the PRINCIPLE OF LEAST PRIVILEGE in the cloud?
options: Grant users/services only the minimum permissions needed for their tasks — never more
Give everyone admin access for convenience
questions.explanation: Least privilege limits damage from any compromise. A read-only role can't delete data even if its credentials leak. Cloud IAM systems support fine-grained policies — use them. Avoid the temptation of broad admin permissions 'just to make things work'.

questions.q: What is a SERVICE ACCOUNT?
options: An identity used by code/services to authenticate to cloud APIs — separate from human user accounts
questions.explanation: Service accounts let your applications authenticate without embedding human credentials. Always use them for service-to-service auth. Rotate their keys regularly. Never check service account keys into git.

questions.q: What is MFA (Multi-Factor Authentication) for cloud accounts?
options: Required additional verification beyond password — essential for ALL human accounts accessing cloud resources
questions.explanation: Cloud account compromises can be catastrophic — attackers spin up massive resources for crypto mining or destroy data. MFA on all cloud accounts (especially admins) is non-negotiable. Use authenticator apps or hardware keys, not SMS where possible.

questions.q: What is FEDERATED IDENTITY in the cloud?
options: Letting users authenticate via an external identity provider (Google, Okta, your own SSO) instead of separate cloud accounts
questions.explanation: Federation centralizes identity management. Add/remove employees in one system (Okta, Azure AD) — access updates everywhere. Eliminates orphaned accounts when people leave. Standard practice for enterprise cloud setups.

questions.q: What is DOCKER?
options: The platform that popularized containers — package applications with their dependencies for consistent execution anywhere
questions.explanation: Docker became synonymous with containers (though Open Container Initiative standards now exist). A Docker image runs the same on a developer's laptop, in CI, in staging, in production. This consistency transformed deployment workflows.

questions.q: What is a DOCKERFILE?
options: A text file with instructions to build a Docker image — like a recipe for the container's contents
questions.explanation: Dockerfiles version-control your environment alongside your code. 'It works on my machine' becomes 'we build the same container everywhere'. Foundational skill for modern cloud development.

questions.q: Why is KUBERNETES so popular for production containers?
options: Automates deployment, scaling, self-healing, rolling updates, networking — at any scale across many containers
questions.explanation: Kubernetes solves the operational complexity of running many containers across many servers. Used by virtually every large tech company. Complex to learn deeply, but managed services hide much complexity.

questions.q: When is Kubernetes OVERKILL?
options: For small/simple apps where a managed PaaS or simple container service would work — K8s complexity isn't always justified
questions.explanation: Don't reach for K8s as your first container platform. Start with simpler options (App Runner, Cloud Run, Fly.io, Render) for most workloads. Adopt K8s when actual complexity demands it — not for resume reasons.

questions.q: What's a SIMPLER alternative to Kubernetes for running containers?
options: AWS App Runner, GCP Cloud Run, Azure Container Apps, Fly.io, Render, Railway — managed simplicity over orchestration power
questions.explanation: The 'managed container' space is competitive. These services handle the orchestration for you. Trade some flexibility for dramatic operational simplicity. Most teams should start here and only adopt K8s when truly justified.

questions.q: What is CI/CD?
options: Continuous Integration / Continuous Deployment — automated testing and deployment of code changes
questions.explanation: CI: every code change is automatically built and tested. CD: passing changes auto-deploy to staging or production. Together: software ships continuously with quality gates. Foundation of modern software delivery. Tools: GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, CircleCI, Jenkins.

questions.q: What is INFRASTRUCTURE AS CODE (IaC)?
options: Defining infrastructure (VMs, networks, databases) in code files instead of clicking through cloud consoles — versionable, reproducible
questions.explanation: IaC tools: Terraform (multi-cloud, dominant), AWS CloudFormation, Azure Bicep, Pulumi. Same infrastructure definition deploys identically in dev/staging/prod. Code-reviewed, version-controlled, auditable. Modern cloud operations standard.

questions.q: What is TERRAFORM?
options: A popular open-source IaC tool — declarative configuration for cloud resources across many providers
questions.explanation: Terraform supports virtually every major cloud and SaaS service through provider plugins. Has become the de facto IaC standard. OpenTofu is the open-source fork following HashiCorp's license changes.

questions.q: What is GITOPS?
options: Managing infrastructure and deployments through git — git becomes the source of truth, changes flow through PRs
questions.explanation: GitOps brings software development practices to operations: peer review (PRs), version history, rollback (git revert), audit trail. Tools (ArgoCD, Flux) sync git state to clusters automatically. Major trend for K8s deployments.

questions.q: What is BLUE-GREEN DEPLOYMENT?
options: Maintain two identical environments (blue and green); deploy new version to inactive one, then switch traffic — instant rollback if needed
questions.explanation: Blue-green minimizes deployment risk. New version warms up in the inactive environment. Traffic switches via load balancer. If problems appear, switch back instantly. Common pattern for high-availability requirements.

questions.q: What is MONITORING in cloud?
options: Watching key metrics, logs, and events to know if systems are healthy and performing well
questions.explanation: Cloud monitoring tools: AWS CloudWatch, Azure Monitor, GCP Cloud Monitoring, Datadog, New Relic, Grafana, Prometheus. Track CPU, memory, errors, latency. Alert when things break. Foundation of any production cloud workload.

questions.q: What's the DIFFERENCE between monitoring and OBSERVABILITY?
options: Monitoring tells you what's broken; observability lets you understand WHY through deep system insight (logs, traces, metrics)
questions.explanation: Observability is monitoring's grown-up sibling. Modern distributed systems are too complex for simple metrics alone. Observability platforms (Datadog, Honeycomb, Grafana, Lightstep) let you investigate novel problems, not just check predefined health checks.

questions.q: What are the THREE PILLARS of observability?
options: Metrics (numbers over time), Logs (events with context), Traces (request paths through systems)
questions.explanation: Metrics: aggregated numbers (CPU, request count, error rate). Logs: timestamped events. Traces: how requests flow through microservices. Together they explain system behavior. OpenTelemetry is the emerging standard for collecting all three.

questions.q: What is ALERTING?
options: Automatically notifying humans when metrics cross thresholds — phone calls, Slack messages, emails for production issues
questions.explanation: Alerts wake people up at 3am. Use them sparingly — only for things genuinely needing human action. Alert fatigue (too many alerts) is dangerous; real alerts get ignored. PagerDuty, OpsGenie, Squadcast manage on-call rotations.

questions.q: What is a SERVICE LEVEL OBJECTIVE (SLO)?
options: A measurable goal for service performance (e.g., '99.9% of requests under 200ms') — gives the team a clear target
questions.explanation: SLOs make reliability concrete. Combined with SLIs (indicators measuring them) and SLAs (commitments to customers), they form a framework popularized by Google's SRE practices. Realistic SLOs guide engineering investment.

questions.q: What is the SHARED RESPONSIBILITY MODEL?
options: Cloud providers secure the infrastructure; customers secure their configurations, access, and what they deploy
questions.explanation: Different services shift the line. With IaaS, customers manage OS and above. With SaaS, providers handle almost everything. Most cloud breaches result from customer-side misconfigurations, not provider failures. Know YOUR responsibilities.

questions.q: What's the BIGGEST source of cloud breaches?
options: Misconfiguration — public buckets, weak IAM, exposed credentials, open security groups
questions.explanation: Public S3 buckets have leaked terabytes. Hardcoded credentials in GitHub get scraped within minutes. Cloud Security Posture Management (CSPM) tools continuously scan for these issues. Make secure defaults the default.

questions.q: What is ENCRYPTION at REST in the cloud?
options: Stored data is encrypted on disk — even if storage hardware is compromised, data is unreadable without keys
questions.explanation: Most cloud services offer encryption at rest by default or with one-click enable. Use it for any sensitive data. Cloud KMS services (AWS KMS, Azure Key Vault, GCP KMS) manage encryption keys securely.

questions.q: What is a SECRET in cloud context?
options: Sensitive values like API keys, passwords, tokens — should never be in code, environment variables in plain text, or logs
questions.explanation: Use secret managers (AWS Secrets Manager, Azure Key Vault, GCP Secret Manager, HashiCorp Vault). Apps fetch secrets at runtime. Never commit secrets to git. Use tools (TruffleHog, GitGuardian) to scan for accidentally committed secrets.

questions.q: What is a CLOUD SECURITY POSTURE MANAGEMENT (CSPM) tool?
options: Continuously scans cloud configurations for security misconfigurations, compliance violations, and risks
questions.explanation: CSPM tools (Wiz, Prisma Cloud, Lacework, native cloud security centers) catch problems humans miss across hundreds of accounts. Essential for medium-large cloud deployments. Find issues before attackers do.

questions.q: Why is CLOUD COST MANAGEMENT important?
options: Cloud bills can grow surprisingly fast without governance — unused resources, oversized instances, data transfer all add up
questions.explanation: Surprise five-figure cloud bills are common. Forgotten test resources, misconfigured services pulling massive data transfer, and runaway autoscaling can drain budgets. Active cost management is essential at any meaningful scale.

questions.q: What's a HIGH-LEVERAGE cloud cost optimization?
options: Right-sizing instances (most workloads use less than they're provisioned for), shutting off unused resources, reserved/savings plans for predictable workloads
questions.explanation: Cost tools (AWS Cost Explorer, Azure Cost Management, GCP billing, third-party like Vantage, CloudHealth) surface waste. The biggest savings often come from rightsizing and removing zombie resources rather than complex optimization tactics.

questions.q: What's a RESERVED INSTANCE or SAVINGS PLAN?
options: Committing to use a certain amount of compute for 1-3 years in exchange for 30-70% discount vs on-demand pricing
questions.explanation: For steady predictable workloads, reservations save significantly. Analyze actual usage before committing. Modern Savings Plans are more flexible than older Reserved Instances. Combine reserved + on-demand for optimal cost.

questions.q: What is FINOPS?
options: Cloud Financial Operations — discipline combining finance, engineering, and ops to manage cloud spending strategically
questions.explanation: FinOps emerged as cloud spending became a major business expense. Cross-functional discipline. FinOps Foundation publishes practices. Mature cloud organizations have dedicated FinOps roles or teams.

questions.q: What's a SURPRISING source of cloud cost?
options: Data egress (data leaving the cloud) — often expensive while ingress is free; cross-region transfers add up too
questions.explanation: Many architectures don't factor in data transfer costs until the bill arrives. Moving lots of data between regions or out to internet can cost hundreds or thousands monthly. Cloudflare's R2 (zero egress) made waves partly for this reason.

questions.q: What is CLOUD-NATIVE design?
options: Designing applications specifically for cloud capabilities — distributed, scalable, resilient, often using containers and managed services
questions.explanation: Cloud-native isn't 'lift and shift' (moving existing apps to cloud unchanged). It's designing to exploit cloud strengths: elasticity, managed services, distributed systems. The CNCF (Cloud Native Computing Foundation) hosts many key projects.

questions.q: What is MICROSERVICES architecture?
options: Building applications as small independent services that communicate over networks — vs one large 'monolith'
questions.explanation: Microservices enable independent deployment, scaling, and technology choices per service. But they introduce complexity (networking, debugging, eventual consistency). Many successful companies (Shopify, GitHub) run modular monoliths instead. Pick based on actual needs, not hype.

questions.q: What is an EVENT-DRIVEN architecture?
options: Components communicate through events — looser coupling than direct API calls; ideal for async, distributed workflows
questions.explanation: Event-driven systems (using SQS, EventBridge, Kafka, Pub/Sub) scale and decouple components. New consumers can subscribe without changing producers. Foundation of many modern serverless and microservices patterns.

questions.q: What is EDGE COMPUTING?
options: Running compute close to users at edge locations worldwide — minimizing latency for geographically distributed users
questions.explanation: Edge platforms (Cloudflare Workers, AWS Lambda@Edge, Vercel Edge Functions) run code in hundreds of cities globally. Perfect for personalization, auth, A/B testing, geo-routing. The future of low-latency global apps.

questions.q: What is JAMSTACK / modern web architecture?
options: JavaScript, APIs, Markup — pre-rendered sites served from CDNs, dynamic features via APIs (often serverless)
questions.explanation: JAMstack patterns dominated modern web architecture. Frameworks like Next.js, Astro, Nuxt embody these ideas. Platforms (Vercel, Netlify, Cloudflare Pages) provide opinionated deployment. Fast, scalable, often inexpensive.

questions.q: How is AI changing cloud computing in 2026?
options: AI workloads drive massive GPU demand, every cloud offers AI/ML services, AI assists developers throughout deployment lifecycle
AI is too expensive for cloud
questions.explanation: AI is the dominant cloud growth driver. Every major cloud offers managed model services (AWS Bedrock, Azure OpenAI Service, Google Vertex AI). GPU shortages make capacity precious. AI assistants reshape developer workflow across the cloud stack.

questions.q: What's MANAGED AI SERVICE in the cloud?
options: Pre-built ML capabilities (vision, speech, language) accessible via API — no need to train your own models
questions.explanation: Examples: AWS Rekognition (vision), Azure Cognitive Services, Google Vision/Speech APIs. Add ML capabilities to apps with simple API calls. Often the right choice over custom models for common tasks.

questions.q: What is a GPU instance in the cloud?
options: VM with attached GPUs — required for training ML models, running large AI workloads, GPU-accelerated computing
questions.explanation: GPUs (NVIDIA dominant) accelerate parallel computation essential for deep learning. Cloud GPU costs are significant (multi-thousands/month for top-tier). High demand for AI training makes capacity scarce; reservations often needed.

questions.q: What's the role of VECTOR DATABASES in cloud AI?
options: Storing embeddings for AI similarity search — foundational for RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation), recommendations, semantic search
questions.explanation: Vector databases (Pinecone, Weaviate, Qdrant, pgvector) became critical AI infrastructure. Cloud providers now offer managed options. Essential for building production RAG systems and any semantic search.

questions.q: What is INFERENCE in cloud AI?
options: Running a trained model to generate predictions — distinct from training; usually the larger ongoing cost
questions.explanation: Training is expensive but one-time. Inference happens with every user request — and costs add up fast at scale. Optimizing inference (model size, batch processing, caching) is increasingly important as AI usage scales.

questions.q: What is HIGH AVAILABILITY (HA)?
options: Designing systems to remain operational despite failures — through redundancy, failover, and fault-tolerant architecture
questions.explanation: HA measured in 'nines' of uptime (99.9% = 'three nines', 99.999% = 'five nines'). Each nine gets exponentially harder and more expensive. Match the target to actual business needs — perfect availability is impossible and costly.

questions.q: What is a REGION vs AVAILABILITY ZONE?
options: Region: geographic area (US-East). AZ: physically isolated data center within a region. Deploying across AZs improves availability
questions.explanation: Regions are geographically separate (US East, Europe West, Asia South). Each region has multiple AZs (data centers in nearby buildings/areas). Multi-AZ protects against single data center failure; multi-region protects against regional disasters.

questions.q: What is a BACKUP STRATEGY?
options: Regular automated copies of critical data, stored separately, tested for restorability — the 3-2-1 rule applies in cloud too
questions.explanation: Cloud doesn't eliminate backup needs. Misconfigurations, accidental deletes, ransomware, account compromises all happen. Cloud-native backup tools simplify the work — but the strategy must still be deliberate. Test restores regularly.

questions.q: What is DISASTER RECOVERY (DR)?
options: Plans and procedures for restoring operations after major incidents (region outage, data loss, breach)
questions.explanation: DR plans define RTO (Recovery Time Objective: how fast to recover) and RPO (Recovery Point Objective: how much data loss is acceptable). Different DR strategies (backup/restore, pilot light, warm standby, multi-region active) trade cost for speed of recovery.

questions.q: What's a COMMON DR mistake?
options: Never testing the disaster recovery plan — untested DR plans usually fail when actually needed
questions.explanation: Untested backups don't restore. Untested failovers fail. Mature organizations run regular DR drills (Game Days). It's uncomfortable; it surfaces gaps before real disasters. The cost of finding flaws in drills is tiny compared to discovering them mid-crisis.

questions.q: What's the BEST way to LEARN cloud computing?
options: Sign up for a free tier, deploy real projects, follow tutorials, take certification courses for structured learning
questions.explanation: AWS Free Tier, Azure Free Account, GCP Free Tier all let beginners deploy real workloads at no cost. Hands-on practice is essential — cloud skills are demonstrated, not just memorized. Build a portfolio project that actually runs in the cloud.

questions.q: What CERTIFICATIONS help start a cloud career?
options: AWS Cloud Practitioner, Azure Fundamentals (AZ-900), GCP Cloud Digital Leader — entry-level certs validating foundational knowledge
questions.explanation: Foundational certs are accessible globally and recognized by employers. Often the first formal credential cloud careers start with. Associate-level certs (AWS Solutions Architect Associate, Azure Administrator) follow for more depth.

questions.q: Which CLOUD provider should you LEARN FIRST?
options: The one used most where you want to work; AWS has biggest market share globally, Azure leads in enterprise, GCP strong in data/AI
questions.explanation: Concepts transfer between clouds. Master one deeply; the others become easier. Check job postings in your target market — they reveal which provider is most in demand locally. AWS is the safest bet for broad applicability globally.

questions.q: What ROLES exist in cloud careers?
options: Cloud Engineer, DevOps, SRE, Cloud Architect, Cloud Security, FinOps, Data Engineer, ML Engineer, Solutions Architect
questions.explanation: Cloud created many specialized roles. SRE (Site Reliability Engineering) merged dev and ops. Cloud Security is its own specialty. FinOps emerged for cost management. Choose based on your interests; almost all are in high demand globally.

questions.q: What's the META-SKILL for long-term cloud careers?
options: Continuous learning — cloud services evolve constantly with new offerings, deprecated features, and shifting best practices
questions.explanation: AWS launches hundreds of new features yearly. What worked five years ago is often outdated now. Successful cloud professionals build learning into their routine — newsletters (Last Week in AWS), conference talks (re:Invent), certifications, blog posts.

questions.q: What's a COMMON cloud mistake?
options: Over-engineering before product-market fit — adopting microservices, Kubernetes, multi-region too early before they're needed
questions.explanation: Many early-stage products would benefit from boring monolithic apps on simple cloud services. Cloud-native complexity is justified at scale, not before. Premature optimization is a major time and money sink. Start simple; complicate when actually needed.

questions.q: What's VENDOR LOCK-IN in cloud?
options: Building dependencies on cloud-specific services that make switching providers difficult or expensive
questions.explanation: Using AWS DynamoDB, Lambda, IAM deeply makes leaving AWS painful. Some lock-in is acceptable for the productivity it brings. Strategic decisions about how much lock-in to accept. Multi-cloud architecture reduces it but adds complexity.

questions.q: What's the COST trade-off of managed services?
options: More expensive per-unit than DIY, but cheaper than the operations cost of doing it yourself — usually worth it
questions.explanation: A managed PostgreSQL costs more than running your own EC2 with PostgreSQL — but factor in the team time, knowledge, on-call burden of managing it yourself. For most teams, managed wins. Specialized teams at scale may justify self-managing.

questions.q: What's a HIDDEN cloud cost?
options: Operational overhead — engineer time spent managing cloud, learning services, troubleshooting issues — not just the bill
questions.explanation: Real cost = AWS bill + team time. A 'cheap' cloud setup that requires constant engineering attention may cost more total than a 'more expensive' managed option. Calculate total cost of ownership, not just invoice line items.

questions.q: What's the RIGHT MINDSET about cloud complexity?
options: Adopt complexity only when its value clearly exceeds the cost — boring solutions often win for years before they need replacement
questions.explanation: Complexity is a liability paid forever. The most successful cloud architectures often look 'boring' — proven services used in standard ways. Save innovation budget for what truly differentiates your product.

questions.q: What's the SERVERLESS trend going forward?
options: Expanding beyond functions: serverless databases, containers (Cloud Run, App Runner), and full applications — pay-per-use becoming default
questions.explanation: Serverless is one direction cloud is heading: more managed, more elastic, more pay-per-actual-use. Databases, containers, and entire application platforms are going serverless. Reduces ops; aligns cost with value.

questions.q: How will AI change cloud OPERATIONS?
options: AI assistants helping with infrastructure design, debugging, cost optimization, security review — AIOps becoming standard
questions.explanation: AI helps cloud engineers move faster: explain configurations, suggest optimizations, write IaC, analyze logs. Doesn't replace expertise but multiplies it. Cloud providers and tools rapidly integrating AI assistants throughout the stack.

questions.q: What is EDGE-FIRST architecture?
options: Designing apps where compute runs as close to users as possible — at edge locations, not centralized regions
questions.explanation: Edge-first reduces latency for global users dramatically. Platforms (Cloudflare Workers, Vercel Edge, Deno Deploy) make it accessible. Best for personalization, auth, A/B tests, simple APIs. The lines between CDN and compute are blurring.

questions.q: What about SUSTAINABILITY in cloud?
options: Major providers committing to renewable energy; sustainable architecture (efficient resource use) increasingly valued
questions.explanation: Data centers consume significant electricity globally. Major providers (Google, Microsoft, AWS) committed to carbon-neutral/negative operations. Region selection now factors in carbon intensity. Tools (AWS Sustainability Pillar, Azure Sustainability Calculator) help measure impact.

questions.q: What's the LONG-TERM trend for cloud computing?
options: More abstraction, more managed services, more AI integration, more global distribution — barrier to building software keeps falling
questions.explanation: Each year, building globally distributed software gets easier and cheaper. Indie developers can build what required entire engineering teams a decade ago. This trend continues — major opportunity for builders willing to learn modern cloud-native patterns.

questions.q: What's a HEALTHY workflow for cloud adoption?
options: Start simple (PaaS/managed services), measure actual needs, complicate only when forced by real requirements
questions.explanation: Most teams over-engineer cloud architectures early. A boring deploy on Render/Vercel/Cloud Run might serve millions of users perfectly. Reach for K8s, multi-region, microservices only when real pain emerges — not for resume reasons.

questions.q: What's a STRONG learning project for cloud?
options: Deploy a real project end-to-end: domain, HTTPS, database, auto-deploy from git, monitoring, basic observability
questions.explanation: End-to-end projects expose all the pieces: networking, secrets, environment management, deployment, monitoring. Pick a simple real project (link shortener, blog, todo API). Build the production-quality version. Lessons learned beat any tutorial.

questions.q: What's a SMART way to manage CLOUD COSTS as a beginner?
options: Set billing alerts immediately, stay within free tiers initially, tear down resources when done experimenting
questions.explanation: Surprise cloud bills are stressful and common. Most providers offer billing alerts — set them in your first hour. Tear down test resources daily. Use cost calculators before deploying paid services. The free tiers alone enable serious learning.

questions.q: What CLOUD SKILL transfers between providers?
options: Core concepts (networking, IAM, compute, storage, databases) transfer easily; specific services and tools differ
questions.explanation: Once you understand cloud concepts deeply on one provider, moving to others is about learning the specific APIs and service names. Concepts compound; tool-specific knowledge depreciates. Focus on fundamentals first.

questions.q: What's the MOST important meta-truth about cloud computing?
options: Cloud removed hardware barriers — now the only limits are imagination, skill, and willingness to learn continuously
Cloud is too complex to learn
questions.explanation: The shift from physical infrastructure to elastic cloud transformed what individuals and small teams can build. A solo developer in any country with an internet connection can deploy software serving millions globally. That's an extraordinary capability. Use it.
